title: Variance Strategy

content.statement: You should not eliminate all variance; you should choose where to absorb and where to suppress it.
content.question: Where in your business should you invite more variance (marketing tests)? Where should you kill it (payouts)?
content.equation: VS = Desired Upside Variance - Undesired Downside Variance
sop: List your core business functions (marketing, payroll, fulfillment, product, compliance) and label each wants-variance or wants-stability.
For wants-variance areas, set a testing budget and run more experiments than feels comfortable.
For wants-stability areas, standardize the process so outcomes are as predictable as possible.
Rate your Desired Upside Variance and Undesired Downside Variance 1-10 for a specific function (see calculation, page 5)
Review the list quarterly, what needed stability at launch might be ready for controlled risk later.
content.explainer: The mistake is treating all variance as bad. Seek it in experiments; kill it in reliability.
questions: Where in your business should you invite more variance (marketing tests)? Where should you kill it (payouts)?
Which part of your business absolutely cannot afford a surprise, no matter how small?
Where have you been playing it safe purely out of habit, in an area that could actually handle more risk?
content.equation_example: Rate Desired Upside Variance 1-10 (how much you want to experiment) and Undesired Downside Variance 1-10 (how much surprise risk you're exposed to) for a specific function. Desired Upside Variance 8, Undesired Downside Variance 3 → VS = 8 - 3 = 5. A higher, positive VS means you're courting the right kind of risk where it pays off; a negative VS means instability is creeping into places that need to be boring and reliable.
content.closing_statement: Some places deserve chaos, others deserve boring.

caption:
Principle Nº 32: Variance Strategy

You should not eliminate all variance; you should choose where to absorb and where to suppress it. The mistake is treating all variance as bad.

Ask yourself: Where in your business should you invite more variance (marketing tests)? Where should you kill it (payouts)?
